messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using only 
the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
- Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0. 
  These components must be present in the vignette but must never be causally 
  connected to any other component — describe them as observable behaviours or 
  states without explaining what produces them or what they produce.
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Rajan, a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms that flare most sharply at night, around bang-like sounds, and whenever he feels he is not in control. He describes a terrorism incident in which a sudden explosion shattered the scene and, in the peak moment of danger, he could do little more than freeze and try to get himself to safety. Even now, the dark of night or waking unexpectedly in darkness takes him back to that helpless moment, and loud bangs from tools, pipes, or a slammed van door can jolt him into immediate panic.

When Rajan speaks about the trauma, he does not tell it as a flowing story. He breaks it into a few isolated moments, each described as if it were a picture he cannot move away from. He points to the same instant of blast, the same fragment of chaos, and says he cannot put the event into words properly. Instead, it comes back to him in stark images that arrive without transition.

His central stuck point is painfully clear: Rajan feels that the event showed him he is “permanently an outsider now,” and that he is somehow to blame because “it happened because of the way I acted.” He also says he feels isolated and set apart from other people, as if something has changed in him that cannot be reversed.

In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building, including client homes and job sites. He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat, and even inside his own house he says he remains on guard. At night he checks the doors and listens for noises when he wakes in the dark. He avoids standing near loud equipment when possible and becomes tense if a sudden bang echoes across a repair site.

A self-reinforcing pattern is evident. Night-time darkness, bang sounds, and moments of feeling out of control bring the trauma back in fragments and images, which intensify his sense that he is unsafe, isolated, and somehow responsible. Those appraisals then push him into more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work. Rajan’s constant checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel even more convincing. His marriage is affected too: he is present physically but preoccupied and watchful, often distracted from ordinary conversation because he is assessing who is near the door and where he could get out quickly.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: while Rajan does scan, check, and remain guarded, the vignette does not explicitly connect triggers causally to a maladaptive strategies component in the required sense, and in any case the graph forbids this edge. No violation marked because there is no explicit causal phrasing in the forbidden direction.
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: the vignette says appraisals push him into scanning/vigilance, but because the target component Maladaptive Strategies must not appear and there is no explicit mention of that component, this forbidden edge is not explicitly stated.
- Memory → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: no explicit causal language connects memory to maladaptive strategies as a named component.
- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: Absent: although threat-related fear leads to vigilance behaviors in the narrative, the vignette does not explicitly state the forbidden component relationship in direct causal terms.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: Absent: the phrase "keeps the event mentally close" is adjacent to checking, but it does not explicitly state the forbidden component Maladaptive Strategies causing Memory.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: Absent: the vignette says repeated return of pictures and bodily alarms makes beliefs more convincing, but this is not an explicit causal statement from Maladaptive Strategies to Negative Appraisals.

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Rajan, a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms that flare most sharply at night, around bang-like sounds, and whenever he feels he is not in control. He describes a terrorism incident in which a sudden explosion shattered the scene and, in the peak moment of danger, he froze, unable to do much beyond trying to get clear of the danger. Even now, waking in the dark or hearing a sharp bang from tools, pipes, or a slammed van door can make him feel as if the danger is back in the room.

When Rajan speaks about the trauma, he does not tell it as a flowing story. He breaks it into a few isolated moments, each described like a picture he cannot move away from. He returns to the same blast, the same flash of chaos, and says he cannot put the event into words properly. What comes back to him is visual and fragmentary, with the images arriving without any smooth narrative.

His central stuck point is painfully clear: Rajan feels that the event showed him he is “permanently an outsider now,” and that “it happened because of the way I acted.” He also says he feels isolated and set apart from other people, as though he no longer belongs in the same world as those around him.

In daily life, Rajan scans for exits as soon as he enters a room or building, including client homes and job sites. He studies people’s faces and body language to work out whether they might be a threat, and even inside his own house he says he remains on guard. At night he checks the doors and listens carefully when he wakes in the dark. He is a plumber, so these reactions arise repeatedly in other people’s homes, on building sites, and in cramped service spaces where he cannot instantly control who is nearby or where the nearest exit is.

A self-reinforcing pattern is evident. Night-time darkness, bang sounds, and moments of feeling out of control bring the trauma back in fragments and images, which intensify his sense that he is unsafe, isolated, and somehow responsible. Those appraisals sit alongside more scanning, more vigilance around faces and exits, and more guardedness at home and at work. Rajan’s checking keeps the event mentally close, and the repeated return of those pictures and bodily alarms makes the same beliefs feel more convincing. His marriage is affected too: he is physically present but preoccupied and watchful, often distracted from ordinary conversation because he is assessing who is near the door and where he could get out quickly.

output:
